questions.question: Explain the difference between absolute and relative pathnames with examples.
keyPoints: Absolute path starts from root (/) and provides complete path location
Relative path starts from current working directory
Example absolute: /home/user/documents/file.txt
Example relative: ./documents/file.txt (when in /home/user)
Absolute paths work from anywhere, relative paths depend on current location

questions.question: Describe the purpose of '.' and '..' in file system navigation.
keyPoints: Single dot (.) represents current directory
Double dot (..) represents parent directory
Used in relative path construction
Example: ./script.sh runs script in current directory
Example: cd .. moves to parent directory

questions.question: Write commands to perform the following: (a) Display current directory (b) Create a directory named 'projects' (c) Navigate to parent directory
keyPoints: (a) pwd - displays current working directory
(b) mkdir projects - creates directory
(c) cd .. - navigates to parent directory
Alternative for (c): cd ../ - same result

questions.question: Explain the significance of ~ (tilde) and - (hyphen) in directory navigation.
keyPoints: ~ represents home directory of current user
~username represents specified user's home directory
- represents previous working directory
cd ~ takes you to your home directory
cd - switches back to directory before last cd

questions.question: If your current directory is /home/student/codes, write commands to: (i) Go to /home/student (ii) Create a directory 'backup' in /home
keyPoints: (i) cd .. (goes to /home/student) OR cd ../.. (from deeper)
(ii) cd /home then mkdir backup OR cd ~/.. then mkdir backup
Alternative: mkdir ../backup creates backup in /home directly

questions.question: What is the root directory? List the common top-level directories in Linux.
keyPoints: Root (/) is the topmost directory in file system hierarchy
All other directories branch from root
Common directories: /bin, /etc, /home, /var, /usr, /opt, /tmp
Root has no parent - cannot go above it

questions.question: Differentiate between mkdir and rmdir commands with examples.
keyPoints: mkdir creates new directories: mkdir myfolder
rmdir removes empty directories: rmdir myfolder
rmdir only works on empty directories
Use rm -r for non-empty directory removal
mkdir -p creates nested directories: mkdir -p a/b/c

questions.question: Explain absolute and relative pathnames in detail. If a user is in /var/log and needs to access /var/www/html/index.html, demonstrate both approaches using appropriate commands.
keyPoints: Absolute path: /var/www/html/index.html (complete from root)
Relative path: ../www/html/index.html (from /var/log)
Explain how ../ moves from /var/log to /var
Then www/html/index.html reaches target
Provide command examples: cd /var/www/html OR cd ../www/html
Discuss pros/cons: absolute is unambiguous, relative is shorter when close

questions.question: Write a shell script that: (a) Creates a directory structure (b) Navigates through it (c) Displays the path at each step. Explain each command used.
keyPoints: Script should create: mkdir -p ~/project/src ~/project/data
Use cd to navigate between directories
Use pwd to display current path at each step
Include comments explaining each command
Show output of pwd after each cd
Demonstrate both absolute and relative path usage

questions.question: Describe the Linux directory hierarchy from root. Explain how path resolution works when navigating using absolute and relative paths with practical examples.
keyPoints: Root (/) is top-level - explain major directories (/home, /etc, /var, /usr)
Absolute path resolution: starts from root, follows each component sequentially
Relative path resolution: starts from current directory
Examples showing navigation from different starting points
Explain special directories: . (current), .. (parent), ~ (home), - (previous)
Practical scenarios demonstrating when to use each type

questions.question: A user is currently in /home/user/Documents/Work. Write commands to: (i) Create a backup directory in /home (ii) Create a temp directory in /tmp (iii) Return to Documents. Show all possible methods.
keyPoints: (i) mkdir /home/backup OR cd ../.. then mkdir backup
(ii) mkdir /tmp/temp OR cd /tmp then mkdir temp
(iii) cd Work OR cd ~/Documents/Work
Alternative using ~: cd ~/Documents/Work for return
Using -: cd - returns to previous directory
Explain each approach's advantages
